elements.sent-2411-001: In a recent paper, Anupam, Chowdhury, and Sen [arXiv:2308.00038] constructed the non-extremal saddle that reproduces the supersymmetric index of the BMPV black hole with three independent charges in the classical limit.
elements.sent-2411-002: This saddle solution is a finite temperature complex solution saturating the BPS bound.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we write this solution in a canonical form in terms of harmonic functions on three-dimensional flat base space, thereby showing that it is supersymmetric.
elements.sent-2411-004: We also show that it exhibits the new form of attraction.
elements.sent-2411-005: String theory has been very successful in counting the microstates of a class of supersymmetric black holes \cite{Strominger:1996sh, Sen:2007qy .}
elements.sent-2411-006: In the large charge limit, the exact microscopic answer for the supersymmetric index, typically known as a Fourier coefficient of a modular form, precisely reproduces the exponential of the Bekenstein-Hawking entropy of the corresponding supersymmetric black hole.
elements.sent-2411-007: This matching is quite remarkable, but it leaves some questions unanswered regarding the relation between the supersymmetric index and the degeneracy.
elements.sent-2411-008: In recent years, a method has been proposed for computing the index directly on the gravity side\cite{2107.09062, 2011.01953 ; see \cite{1810.11442 for earlier work on AdS black holes.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Using this approach, the result of the index computation has been shown to agree with the Bekenstein-Hawking entropy~\cite{Boruch:2023gfn,Hegde:2023jmp,2401.13730, 2402.03297, 2306.07322,Anupam:2023yns , including logarithmic corrections \cite{2306.07322,Anupam:2023yns , and very recently including higher derivative corrections \cite{2402.03297, Hegde:2024bmb .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Much of these developments have focused on four-dimensional black holes.
elements.sent-2411-011: The aim of this paper is to further explore these ideas in five dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-012: There are already some key results in the literature in 5D: (i) Anupam, Chowdhury, and Sen (ACS) in Ref.~\cite{Anupam:2023yns constructed the saddle solution for the gravitational path integral that computes the index for the five-dimensional BMPV black hole with three independent charges, (ii) Ref.~\cite{Hegde:2023jmp showed that the ACS saddle solution with equal charges is indeed supersymmetric in five-dimensional minimal supergravity, and very recently (iii) in Ref.~\cite{Cassani:2024kjn the authors have computed the on-shell supergravity action for the five-dimensional saddle solution using equivariant localisation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: In this paper, we focus on the five-dimensional ACS saddle solution in U(1)$^3$ supergravity with three-independent charges as constructed in~\cite{Anupam:2023yns .}
elements.sent-2411-014: The rest of the paper is organised as follows.
elements.sent-2411-015: The ACS construction does not manifest the fact that the saddle solution is supersymmetric.
elements.sent-2411-016: In section \ref{sec:ACS_BW , we write the solution in a canonical form in terms of harmonic functions on three-dimensional flat base space, thereby showing that it is supersymmetric.}
elements.sent-2411-017: In section \ref{sec:attractor , via the 4D-5D connection, we show that the ACS saddle solution exhibits the new form of attraction \cite{Boruch:2023gfn , i.e., the coefficients at the poles of the harmonic functions are temperature and moduli independent and are determined by the solutions to the attractor equations for the BMPV black hole charges.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: We close with a brief discussion in section \ref{sec:conclusions .}
elements.section-2411-002: The ACS saddle solution in the Bena-Warner form
elements.sent-2411-020: The bosonic fields are the metric and a 3-form potential ${\cal A}$.
elements.sent-2411-021: The bosonic Lagrangian is \be {\cal L _{11 = R_{11 \star_{11 1 - \frac{1 {2 {\cal F \wedge \star_{11 {\cal F - \frac{1 {6 {\cal F \wedge {\cal F \wedge {\cal A , \ee where $R_{11}$ denotes the eleven-dimensional Ricci scalar, ${\cal F = d {\cal A}$, and $\star_{11}$ is the eleven-dimensional Hodge star.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: We are interested in five-dimensional theory obtained by dimensional reduction on $T^6$ for the following form of the bosonic fields, \bea ds_{11 ^2 = ds^2_{5 + h^1 \left(dz_1^2 + dz_2^2 \right) + h^2 \left( dz_3^2 + dz_4^2 \right) + h^3 \left( dz_5^2 + dz_6^2 \right), {\cal A = A^1 \wedge dz_1 \wedge dz_2 + A^2 \wedge dz_3 \wedge dz_4 + A^3 \wedge dz_5 \wedge dz_6.}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: As a result, we can regard $ds^2_{5}$ as a five-dimensional metric and $h^I$ and $A^I$ as five-dimensional scalars and vectors, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-024: To ensure that the five-dimensional metric is the Einstein-frame metric we also assume that the scalars $h^I$ obey the constraint \be h^1 h^2 h^3 = 1.
elements.sent-2411-025: The Maxwell field strengths are $F^I = dA^I$.
elements.sent-2411-026: The ACS saddle solution is conjectured to be a supersymmetric solution to this five-dimensional theory.
elements.sent-2411-027: Building upon the seminal work of~\cite{Gauntlett:2002nw , supersymmetric solutions to this theory have been studied in great detail~\cite{Bena:2004de, Gauntlett:2004qy, Bena:2005va .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: It is well known that a large class of supersymmetric solutions of this theory can be written in a canonical form in terms of 8 harmonic functions on three-dimensional flat base space; see \cite{Bena:2007kg for a review and further references.}
elements.sent-2411-029: We call this canonical form the Bena-Warner form.
elements.sent-2411-030: A key aim of this paper is to write the ACS solution in the Bena-Warner form.
elements.sent-2411-031: We can think of the ACS solution as the Cvetic-Youm solution \cite{Cvetic:1996xz with certain constraints on the parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-032: The Cvetic-Youm solution is labelled by six parameters: mass parameter $m$, rotation parameters $a, b$, and charge parameters $\delta_I, I = 1,2,3$.
elements.sent-2411-033:
In \cite{Wu:2011gq , Wu observed that the Cvetic-Youm metric can be written in the following simple form,  ea \diff s^2 = ({\cal H _1 {\cal H _2 {\cal H _3)^{1/3} igg[ -\diff t^2 + rac{\Sigma r^2 { ar{\Delta _r}\, \diff r^2 +\Sigma\, \diff 	heta^2 +(r^2+a^2)\sin^2	heta\, \diff \phi^2 
onumber +(r^2+b^2)\cos^2	heta\, \diff \psi^2 + rac{2ms_1^2 { {\cal H _1 \Sigma (s_1^2-s_2^2)(s_1^2-s_3^2) k_1^2 
onumber + rac{2ms_2^2 { {\cal H _2 \Sigma (s_2^2-s_1^2)(s_2^2-s_3^2) k_2^2 + rac{2ms_3^2 { {\cal H _3 \Sigma (s_3^2-s_1^2)(s_3^2-s_2^2) k_3^2  igg] \, , \eea where  ea k_I =  rac{s_Ic_1c_2c_3 {c_I}\Big( rac{c_I^2 {c_1c_2c_3}\diff t -a\sin^2	heta\, \diff \phi -b\cos^2	heta\, \diff \psi\Big) 
onumber + rac{c_Is_1s_2s_3 {s_I}\Big(b\sin^2	heta\, \diff \phi +a\cos^2	heta\, \diff \psi\Big) \, , \eea and  ea  ar{\Delta _r = (r^2+a^2)(r^2+b^2) -2mr^2 \, , {\cal H _I = 1 + rac{2ms_I^2 {\Sigma}\, , \qquad \Sigma = r^2 + a^2 \cos^2 	heta + b^2 \sin^2 	heta \, , \eea together with $c_I = \cosh\delta_I $ and $s_I = \sinh\delta_I $.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: The vectors and the scalars supporting the solution take the form \be A^I = \frac{2m { {\cal H _I \Sigma k_I , \qquad \qquad h^I = \frac{{\cal H _I { ({\cal H _1{\cal H _2{\cal H _3)^{1/3 .}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: This requires, \bea \delta_I = \frac{1 {2 \sinh^{-1 \frac{Q^{(I) {m , a-b = \frac{J_{3L {2m(c_1 c_2 c_3 +s_1 s_2 s_3) , a+b = \frac{J_{3R {2m(c_1 c_2 c_3 -s_1 s_2 s_3) , \eea as we take the $m\to0$ limit.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: In the $m\to0$ limit, $a-b$ goes to zero as \be a-b \simeq \frac{J_{3L \sqrt{m {\sqrt{2Q^{(1) Q^{(2) Q^{(3) , \ee and $a+b$ diverges as \be a+b \simeq \frac{\sqrt{2 J_{3R \sqrt{Q^{(1) Q^{(2) Q^{(3) {\sqrt{m (Q^{(1) Q^{(2) + Q^{(2) Q^{(3) + Q^{(1) Q^{(3) ) .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Since in the $m\to0$ limit $a$ and $b$ diverge, so do $r_\pm$.
elements.sent-2411-039: In order to have the horizon of the final black hole at a finite location Ref.~\cite{Anupam:2023yns defines, \begin{equation}\rho^2 \equiv r^2 - \frac12 (r_+^2+r_-^2),\end{equation} and writes the final metric in $(t, \rho, \theta, \phi, \psi)$ coordinates with $Q^{(1) , Q^{(2) , Q^{(3) , J_{3L}$, and $J_{3R}$ as the five parameters.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Moreover, they restrict the parameter ranges to $Q^{(I) > 0$ and $J_{3L}$ real such that \be D:= \sqrt{4 Q^{(1) Q^{(2) Q^{(3) - J_{3L ^2 > 0, \ee and $J_{3R}$ lie along the positive imaginary axis, \be -iJ_{3R > 0.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The vectors and the scalars take the form, \bea A^I = \frac{1 {\Delta_I}\Big\{2\Qi Q_P\, \diff t + \zeta_I \sin^2\theta\, \diff \phi + \widetilde \zeta_I \cos^2\theta\, \diff \psi\Big\}, \eea where \be \zeta_I =2J_{3R}\frac{Q_T {\Qi -(J_{3L +J_{3R )Q_P, \quad \quad \widetilde \zeta_I = 2J_{3R}\frac{Q_T {\Qi +(J_{3L -J_{3R )Q_P, \ee \be \Delta_I = \cos(2\theta)J_{3L J_{3R +2(\Qi+\rho^2)Q_P, \ee and the scalars are \be h^I = \frac{\Delta_I {(\Delta_1 \Delta_2 \Delta_3)^{1/3 .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Metric \eqref{emetriclimit can be written in the form, \begin{equation}\diff s^2= -f^2(\diff t +\omega_\phi \diff \phi +\omega_\psi \diff \psi)^2+f^{-1} \diff s^2_{\mathrm{4d-base}}, \label{5dmetric}\end{equation} with \bea f = \frac{\cos (2 \theta ) J_{3L J_{3R +2 \rho^2 Q_P {2 \, \Delta^{1/3 \, Q_P , \wf = \frac{Q_P\, \sin^2 \theta\,[4 \jr Q_T+ 2(\jr+\jl) Q_P \, \rho^2 +\jl \jr \, (\jr+\jl ) \cos(2\theta)] {(2Q_P^2\, \rho^2+\jl\jr\,\cos(2\theta))^2 , \nonumber \ws = \frac{Q_P\, \cos^2 \theta\,[4 \jr Q_T+ 2(\jr-\jl) Q_P \, \rho^2 +\jl \jr \, (\jr-\jl ) \cos(2\theta)] {(2Q_P^2\, \rho^2+\jl\jr\,\cos(2\theta))^2 .}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: We also note that the four-dimensional base metric $\diff s^2_{\mathrm{4d-base }$ is Ricci flat.}
elements.sent-2411-045: The base metric $\diff s^2_{\mathrm{4d-base }$ can be written in the Gibbons-Hawking form in the same way as in \cite{Hegde:2023jmp , \begin{equation}\diff s^2_{\mathrm{4d-base}} = V^{-1}(\diff z+A)^2+ V\diff s^2_{\mathrm{3d-base}}, \label{GibbHawk}\end{equation} where \be z = \psi - \phi, \qquad \varphi = \phi + \psi, \ee and the three-dimensional base metric $\diff s^2_{\mathrm{3d-base }$ with coordinates $(\rho, \theta, \varphi)$ is flat.}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: The function $V$ in equation \eqref{GibbHawk reads, \begin{equation}V=\frac{4\,(\rho^2+c \, d\, \cos 2\theta)}{\rho^4+(1-c^2) \, d^2 \, \cos^2 2\theta}, \label{harmonicV}\end{equation} with the 1-form $A$ satisfying \begin{equation}\star_3 \diff A=\diff V, \label{stardV}\end{equation} where $\star_3$ is the Hodge star in three-dimensions with the three-dimensional base metric $\diff s^2_{\mathrm{3d-base }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: The three-dimensional base metric takes the form, \begin{equation}\diff s^2_{3d-base}= \frac1{4}(\rho^4+(1-c^2) \, d^2 \cos^2 2\theta)\bigg\{\frac{\rho^2}{2 (\rho^4+(1-c^2)\, d^2)}\, \diff\rho^2+\diff \theta^2\bigg\} \nonumber + \frac{1}{16}(\rho^4+(1-c^2)\,d^2) \sin^2 2\theta\, \diff\varphi^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-049: To arrive at the cartesian form for the three-dimensional base, \be \diff s^2_{\mathrm{3d-base = \diff y_1^2 + \diff y_2^2 + \diff y_3^2, \ee we need to do the following coordinate transformation, \bea y_1 = \frac{1 {4 \, \sqrt{\rho^4+(1-c^2)d^2 \, \sin 2\theta \, \sin \varphi, y_2 = \frac{1 {4 \, \sqrt{\rho^4+(1-c^2)d^2 \, \sin 2\theta\, \cos \varphi, y_3 = \frac{1 {4 \, \rho^2 \, \cos 2\theta.. \eea.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: The full solution above can now readily be written in the Bena-Warner form in terms of 8 harmonic functions $\{V, K^I, L_I, M\}$.
elements.sent-2411-051: We quickly review the Bena-Warner form of the solution and then write the 8 harmonic functions for the ACS solution.
elements.sent-2411-052: The metric takes the form, \be \diff s^2 = -f^2(\diff t +k)^2+f^{-1 \diff s^2_{\mathrm{4d-base , \ee with the four-dimensional base metric given in \eqref{GibbHawk and \eqref{stardV , and where the one-form $k$ on the four-dimensional base space is, \be k=\mu(\diff z +A) +\omega.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: In terms of the $\{\rho,\theta,\varphi\}$ coordinates, $r_N,r_S$ take the form, \begin{equation}r_N=\frac14(\rho^2+i\sqrt{1-c^2} \, d \cos 2\theta ), r_S=\frac14(\rho^2-i\sqrt{1-c^2} \, d \cos 2\theta ).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-054: Note that given our parameter restrictions \eqref{restriction-1 --\eqref{restriction-2 , the pole locations are real.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The 8 harmonic functions are, \begin{equation}K^I = \frac{k^I_N}{r_N}+ \frac{k^I_S}{r_S}, V = \frac{v_N}{r_N}+\frac{v_S}{r_S}, L_I= 1+ \frac{l_{IN}}{r_N}+\frac{l_{IS}}{r_S}, M= \frac{m_N}{r_N}+\frac{m_S}{r_S},\end{equation} where, \begin{equation}\label{coe-harmonic-1} v_N = (v_S)^* = \frac12\bigg(1+i\frac {J_{3L}}{D}\bigg) , k^I_N = (k^I_S)^* = \frac i{2}\frac{Q^{(1)}Q^{(2)}Q^{(3)}}{\Qi D} , m_N = (m_S)^* = - \frac1{32} \left(J_{3L}+\frac{i}{2}\left(D - \frac{J_{3L}^2}{D}\right)\right), l_{IN} = (l_{IS})^* = \frac{ 1}{8}\Qi \left(1-i \frac {J_{3L}}{D}\right),\label{coe-harmonic-4}\end{equation} with $D$ given in \eqref{restriction-1 .}
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}\label{coe-harmonic-1} v_N = (v_S)^* = \frac12\bigg(1+i\frac {J_{3L}}{D}\bigg) , k^I_N = (k^I_S)^* = \frac i{2}\frac{Q^{(1)}Q^{(2)}Q^{(3)}}{\Qi D} , m_N = (m_S)^* = - \frac1{32} \left(J_{3L}+\frac{i}{2}\left(D - \frac{J_{3L}^2}{D}\right)\right), l_{IN} = (l_{IS})^* = \frac{ 1}{8}\Qi \left(1-i \frac {J_{3L}}{D}\right),\label{coe-harmonic-4}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-056: These harmonic functions are our first key result of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-057: Having written the ACS solution in the Bena-Warner form, it follows that the solution is supersymmetric.
elements.sent-2411-058: The Killing spinors for the general Bena-Warner class of solutions were given in \cite{Bena:2004de .}
elements.sent-2411-059: We note that the Killing spinors constructed in \cite{Hegde:2023jmp are also of the same form.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The non-real nature of the solution does not introduce additional subtleties.
elements.section-2411-003: The attractor nature of the ACS saddle
elements.sent-2411-061: In this section, we show that the ACS saddle exhibits the new form of attraction~\cite{Boruch:2023gfn , i.e., the coefficients at the poles of the harmonic functions \eqref{coe-harmonic-1 --\eqref{coe-harmonic-4 are determined by the solution to the attractor equations for the BMPV black hole charges.}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: To show this, it is useful to consider further (formal) dimensional reduction to four dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-063: This gives the ${\cal N =2$ STU model.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Four-dimensional STU model is governed by a prepotential function $F$ depending on four complex scalars $X^M, M=0,1, 2,\,3$, \be F(X) = - \frac{X^1 X^2 X^3 {X^0 .}}
elements.sent-2411-065: It is convenient to work with four complex scalars $X^M$ to begin with and later introduce a gauge fixing.
elements.sent-2411-066: The 4D Lagrangian in a standard set of conventions can be obtained following the formalism reviewed in \cite{Mohaupt:2000mj .}
elements.sent-2411-067: In this formalism at several stages one needs to take the complex conjugation.
elements.sent-2411-068: In the solution that we are considering the metric and other fields also take complex values.
elements.sent-2411-069: In taking the complex conjugate, one should remember that this operation does not act on the solution, i.e., even if the metric is complex, the complex conjugate of $g_{\mu\nu}$ in the supergravity formalism give us back $g_{\mu\nu}$.
elements.sent-2411-070: We define, $ F_M ={\partial F\over \partial X^M , $ and work in the so-called $D$-gauge~\cite{Mohaupt:2000mj , \be i (\bar{X ^M F_M - \bar{F _M X^M ) = 1, \ee to ensure that the Einstein-Hilbert term is canonically normalised.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: The relation between the five-dimensional and the four-dimensional fields is via dimensional reduction.
elements.sent-2411-072: We write the five-dimensional metric as, \begin{equation}ds^2_5 = \wt f^{2}(\diff z - \check A^0)^2 + \wt f^{-1} \diff s^2_4, \label{sol-5d-1}\end{equation} and the vectors as, \begin{equation}A^I = \chi^I(\diff z - \check A^0)+ \check A^I, \qquad I = 1, 2, 3. \label{sol-5d-2}\end{equation} Note the minus signs in front of $\check A^0$ terms.
elements.sent-2411-073: In writing these equations, we assume that that no five-dimensional field depends on the coordinate $z$.
elements.sent-2411-074: As a result, there is a four-dimensional description.
elements.sent-2411-075: The dimensionally reduced Lagrangian reads, see, e.g., \cite{Virmani:2012kw , \bea \mathcal{L _4 = R \star_4 \mathbf{1 - \frac{1 {2 G_{IJ \star_4 \diff h^I \wedge \diff h^J - \frac{3 {2\wt f^2}\star_4 \diff \wt f \wedge \diff \wt f \nonumber - \frac{\wt f^3 {2}\star_4 \check F^0 \wedge \check F^0 - \frac{1 {2 \wt f^2 G_{IJ \star_4 \diff \chi^I \wedge \diff \chi^J - \frac{\wt f {2 G_{IJ \star_4 (\check F^I -\chi^I \check F^0) \wedge (\check F^J -\chi^J \check F^0) -\frac{1 {2 C_{IJK}\chi^I \check F^J \wedge \check F^K + \frac{1 {2 C_{IJK}\chi^I \chi^J \check F^0 \wedge \check F^K - \frac{1 {6 C_{IJK \chi^I \chi^J \chi^K \check F^0 \wedge \check F^0\, .}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The scalars $\chi^I$ and $h^I$ combine to form the complex scalars in the STU model as \be z^I = \frac{X^I {X^0 = \chi^I + i \wt f h^I, \ee in the D-gauge.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: For the class of solutions we are interested in, the four-dimensional metric takes the form \be \diff s^2 = - e^{2g (\diff t + \omega)^2 + e^{-2 g \diff s^2_{\mathrm{3d-base , \ee where $\diff s^2_{\mathrm{3d-base }$ is the metric on the three-dimensional flat base space and $\omega$ is a one-form on the three-dimensional base space\footnote{This $\omega$ and the $\omega$ introduced in \eqref{omega-1 and \eqref{omega-2 are the same.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Here we are following the conventions and notation of \cite{Mohaupt:2000mj, 0009234 , which are slightly different from \cite{Boruch:2023gfn, 2402.03297 .}}
elements.sent-2411-081: We define, \be Y^M = e^{-g \bar{h X^M, \ee where $h$ is a position dependent phase that enter the description of the solutions.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The homogeneity of the pre-potential function, \be G(Y):= F(X (Y)), \ee implies, \be G_M := \frac{\partial {\partial Y^M G(Y) = e^{-g \bar{h F_M.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: In terms of the harmonic functions, a rich class of half-BPS solutions of the STU model take the form~\cite{9705169 , \be e^{-2g = \Sigma = -i (Y^M \bar G_M - \bar{Y ^M G_M), \ee such that \bea \Sigma^2 = - \left(\sum_{M=0 ^{3 H^M H_M\right)^2 + \sum_{I, J, K, J', K' = 1 ^{3 C_{IJK H^J H^K C^{IJ'K' H_{J' H_{K' + 4 H^0 H_1 H_2 H_3 \nonumber - 4 H_0 H^1 H^2 H^3.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: The generalized stabilization equations \eqref{GSE-1 --\eqref{GSE-2 give rise to the attractor equations in the $r \to 0$ limit, \bea Y^M_* - \bar Y^M_* = i P^M, G_M{ _* - \bar G_M{ _* = i Q_M, \eea where \bea Y^M = \frac{Y^M_* {r + \OO(1), G_M = \frac{G_M{ _* {r + \OO(1), \bar Y^M = \frac{\bar Y^M_* {r + \OO(1), \bar G_M = \frac{\bar G_M{ _* {r + \OO(1), \eea as $r\to0$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Given the charges $\{P^M, Q_M\}$ the attractor equations \eqref{attractor-1 --\eqref{attractor-2 determine $\{ Y^M_*, \bar Y^M_*, G_M{ _*, \bar G_M{ _* \}.$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-086: For the solutions we are interested in, the harmonic functions have only two poles, and they are taken as \be H = h + \frac{\gamma_N {r_N + \frac{\gamma_S {r_S , \ee where $r_N$ and $r_S$ are the distances to the point $\{y^1, y^2, y^3\}$ from the north and the south poles, cf.~\eqref{north-pole-location-1 --\eqref{south-pole-location-1 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: The charges $\gamma_N^M$, $\gamma_{NM}$, $\gamma_S^M$ and $\gamma_{SM}$ at the poles of the harmonic functions are determined from the equations \cite{Boruch:2023gfn : \bea \gamma_N^M + \gamma_S^M = P^M, \gamma_{NM + \gamma_{SM = Q_M\, , \eea where $P^M$ and $Q_M$ are the electric and magnetic charges carried by the black hole, and by demanding that, \bea Y^M = {i \gamma_N^M\over r_N + \OO(1), G_M = {i\gamma_{NM}\over r_N +\OO(1), \bar Y^M = \OO(1)\, , \bar G_M = \OO(1)\, , \eea as $r_N \to 0$, and \bea Y^M = \OO(1), G_M = \OO(1)\, , \bar Y^M = -{i \gamma_S^M\over r_S +\OO(1)\, , \bar G_M = -{i\gamma_{SM}\over r_S +\OO(1)\, , \eea as $r_S\to 0$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: In this way, the generalized stabilization equations \eqref{GSE-1 --\eqref{GSE-2 are satisfied at the poles.}}
elements.sent-2411-089: It then follows from \eqref{egammasum_1 --\eqref{egammasum_2 and \eqref{attractor-1 --\eqref{attractor-2 that for the given charges $\{P^M, Q_N\}$, \be \{i\gamma_N^M, i\gamma_{NM , -i\gamma_S^M, -i\gamma_{SM}\} =\{ Y^M_*, G_M{ _*, \bar Y^M_*, \bar G_M{ _* \}.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: The ACS saddle solution computes the supersymmetric index for the BMPV black hole with three independent charges.
elements.sent-2411-091: In order to obtain the coefficients at the poles for the harmonic functions, \be \{\gamma_N^M, \gamma_{NM , \gamma_S^M, \gamma_{SM}\}, \ee from the new attractor mechanism reviewed in the previous section, we need to view the 5D asymptotically flat BMPV black hole from the 4D perspective.}
elements.sent-2411-092: The 4D-5D connection \cite{Gaiotto:2005gf allows us to identify the 4D charges $\{P^M, Q_M\}$ for the 5D BMPV black hole.}
elements.sent-2411-093: We have\footnote{The factors of $\sqrt{2}$ are there because there is a $\sqrt{2}$ in our Eq.~\eqref{AM-formula .}}
elements.sent-2411-094: The origin of this factor is the difference in the normalisation of the vector fields.
elements.sent-2411-095: In the $N=2$ supergravity formalism, the vectors are typically normalised so that in the Lagrangian the kinetic term for a vector is $-\frac{1 {2 F_{\mu \nu F^{\mu \nu}$ (see e.g., \cite{Mohaupt:2000mj, 0009234 ) when the coefficient of the Einstein-Hilbert term is unity.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: In our case the vector kinetic terms are normalised (in a more standard manner) in Eq.~\eqref{STUaction as $-\frac{1 {4 F_{\mu \nu F^{\mu \nu}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: These expressions perfectly match with the coefficients of the harmonic functions computed in the previous section \eqref{coe-harmonic-1 --\eqref{coe-harmonic-4 upon taking into account the normalisation \eqref{harmonic_normalisation1 --\eqref{harmonic_normalisation4 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: This matching is our second key result.
elements.sent-2411-099: In this paper, we have written the Anupam, Chowdhury, and Sen (ACS) non-extremal saddle solution \cite{Anupam:2023yns in the Bena-Warner form that manifests its supersymmetric nature.}
elements.sent-2411-100: Using the 4D-5D connection, we have also shown that the saddle solution exhibits the new form of attraction.
elements.sent-2411-101: Our results offer opportunities for future work.
elements.sent-2411-102: In refs.~\cite{2402.03297, Hegde:2024bmb , the agreement between the entropy and index was shown to hold for half BPS black holes in $N=2$ supergravity with higher derivative terms in four-dimensions.}
elements.sent-2411-103: The corresponding five-dimensional theories with higher derivative terms are well studied \cite{Hanaki:2006pj, Banerjee:2011ts .}
elements.sent-2411-104: As a future direction, it will be useful to extend the analysis of \cite{Hegde:2024bmb to five-dimensions.}
elements.sent-2411-105: We hope to report on this in our future work.
elements.sent-2411-106: We thank Subramanya Hegde, Ashoke Sen, and P Shanmugapriya for discussions.. S.A.
elements.sent-2411-108: In this paper, we have written the Anupam-Chowdhury-Sen (ACS) non-extremal saddle solution \cite{Anupam:2023yns in the Bena-Warner form that manifests its supersymmetric nature.}
elements.sent-2411-109: Since for the corresponding extremal black holes the 4D-5D connection is well established~\cite{Gaiotto:2005gf , it is perhaps not surprising the 4D-5D connection also allows us to relate the saddles that contribute to the 4D and 5D indices.}
elements.sent-2411-110: It is now well appreciated that the AdS$_2$/CFT$_1$ correspondence gives a prescription for computing the degeneracy of black hole microstates in terms of the path integral over fields living in the near-horizon region.
elements.sent-2411-111: The near-horizon path integral receives contributions only from a class of configurations which are invariant under a subgroup of the supersymmetry transformations~\cite{Banerjee:2009af, Iliesiu:2022kny, Sen:2023dps .}
elements.sent-2411-112: Examples of such saddle points have been identified.
elements.sent-2411-113: Our current understanding of the saddle points of the path integral that uses asymptotically flat geometry to compute the supersymmetric index is not at the same level.
elements.sent-2411-114: In this paper, we have taken a small step towards improving our understanding of the saddle points, where we have constructed the Killing spinors and have established some key features related to the attractor mechanism for the dominant saddle that contributes to the 5D black hole index.
elements.sent-2411-115: We hope that in the future localisation techniques will allow to show that the path integral receives contributions from a special class of configurations; a sub-class of them may be related to our construction in a direct way (e.g., as $\mathbb{Z _k$ orbifolds).}
elements.sent-2411-116: In this context, the results reported in this paper may be significant.
elements.sent-2411-117: Our results offer other opportunities for future works, too.
